Title: Search intensifies for missing hiker as operation enters critical phase
Article:
TAPAH: The search and rescue (SAR) operation for female hiker Jaslinda Saludin, 49, who went missing while climbing Gunung Batu Putih on May 23, has entered a critical phase after she was lost for more than 72 hours.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department director Datuk Sayani Saidon said the SAR operation had been intensified, with additional personnel deployed and the search area expanded to locations where the victim may seek food and water.
She said the search would now focus on areas along Sungai Ayer Busok, where the victim may have gone in search of water.
"We can say this is a critical period, but given the victim's experience, she should be able to survive while the SAR operation is ongoing.
"With her experience, we believe she will attempt to find a water source to sustain herself and is highly likely to head towards Sungai Ayer Busok, which is close to Gunung Batu Putih," she said at a press conference after visiting the incident command post at Pos Kuala Poh today.
Sayani said 36 personnel from the Fire and Rescue Department and police were deployed today to the Kem Sukaneka and Gunung Rayu areas, assisted by the General Operations Force, Forestry Department and Forestry Mountain Guides.
She said the department had also cleared a winching point area measuring about 9.3sq m at Kem Salai Matahari to allow for quick evacuation or logistical support if a helicopter was required.
She said this would ease the movement of the rescue team, as the current winching points at Bukit Batu Putih and Bukit Berapit 2 were too far from the primary search area.
Sayani said the steep and mountainous terrain posed a challenge for the rescue team, with travel to some search locations taking between seven and eight hours.
Jaslinda reportedly began the Trans Spencer Chapman climb at 2am on May 23 with 13 other hikers and two mountain guides via the Pos Gedung-Gunung Bah Gading-Gunung Batu Putih-Kuala Woh route.
She and another hiker, Mohd Hanafi Neikmad, 41, reportedly experienced health issues and stopped. However, Jaslinda later continued alone to the mountain summit and was last seen by a mountain guide at 7.30am on May 24.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Government drafting initiatives to help media practitioners navigate AI, economic challenges
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government, through the Communications Ministry, is formulating initiatives to help media practitioners navigate current challenges, including economic uncertainty and the rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AI).
Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said this year's National Journalists' Day (Hawana) comes amid concerns among media practitioners over their jobs and future in the industry.
"This year we are celebrating Journalists' Day in an atmosphere where many may be worried about their jobs and their future as a result of AI and economic issues, he said in his message in conjunction with the Hawana 2026 celebration today.
"But Insya-Allah, on the government's part, especially the Communications Ministry, several matters are being looked into to assist friends in the media industry, he said.
Fahmi said the existence of a free and ethical media forms the cornerstone of the country's democratic system.
"Free and ethical media are vital, not only for the country's democratic system, but also for the future of Malaysia," he said.
With the theme "Media Integrity Strengthens Credibility", the Hawana 2026 celebration will take place at the PICCA Convention Centre @ Arena Butterworth in Penang on June 20.
The event is expected to be officiated by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
It is expected to bring together about 1,000 media practitioners from both domestic and international backgrounds, serving as a strategic platform to generate fresh ideas and set the direction of contemporary journalism.
May 29 was gazetted as National Journalists' Day in conjunction with the publication of the first edition of the Utusan Melayu newspaper on May 29, 1939, to honour the contributions and services of media practitioners and veteran journalists in the nation's development.
Organised by the ministry with the Malaysian National News Agency (Bernama) as the implementing agency, Hawana serves as the pinnacle platform in recognising the contributions, dedication and professionalism of media practitioners in Malaysia.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI giant Anthropic reaches near-trillion dollar valuation
Article:
SAN FRANCISCO: Artificial intelligence company Anthropic said Thursday it had raised US$65 billion in a new funding round that values the Claude maker at US$965 billion, more than its archrival OpenAI, the maker of ChatGPT.
The latest fundraising round confirms Anthropic's place as one of the most significant players in AI, with the startup led by Dario Amodei having drawn fans for its coding powers and state-of-the-art models.
Anthropic's rise came by doubling down on delivering generative AI to enterprise clients rather than general users, the path initially chosen by rival OpenAI.
Founded by former OpenAI employees, including chief executive officer Amodei, Anthropic has a special focus on AI safety even as it rushes out new products to remain in the AI race.
"This funding will help us serve the historic demand we are experiencing, stay at the research frontier, and bring Claude to more of the places where work happens," said Krishna Rao, Anthropic's chief financial officer.
Anthropic's near-trillion-dollar valuation puts it ahead of OpenAI, which was valued at US$852 billion in March. Both companies could go public as early as this year.
Ahead of them, SpaceX, owned by Elon Musk and which absorbed his AI company, xAI, in February, could see shares begin trading as early as June 12, targeting a valuation of approximately US$1.75 trillion in what would be the largest IPO in history.
Anthropic's round was led by major Silicon Valley venture capitalists Altimeter Capital, Dragoneer, Greenoaks and Sequoia Capital.
It also included US$15 billion in previously committed investments from cloud giants, including US$5 billion from Amazon.
Semiconductor firms Micron, Samsung and SK hynix — whose products are crucial to the technology — also participated as strategic infrastructure partners.
The company said Claude is now the first frontier AI model available across all three of the world's largest cloud platforms — Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud and Microsoft Azure.
The funding comes as Anthropic navigates a high-profile legal dispute with the Pentagon, having sued the United States Department of Defense after it designated the company a supply chain risk — a move Anthropic called unconstitutional retaliation for its refusal to grant the military unfettered access to its AI models.
Mythos is Anthropic's powerful, next-generation AI model with unprecedented cybersecurity capabilities.
This has led Anthropic to restrict its access to security partners rather than releasing it to the public.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unrecognised Alexandria University pharmacy programme leaves Malaysian students in limbo
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Pharmacy Board (PBM) was not consulted over the Higher Education Ministry's offering of the clinical pharmacy (PharmD) programme at Alexandria University in Egypt.
The board's Pharmacy Services Programme Division said this in response to concerns that the university's PharmD programme is not recognised in Malaysia.
The issue involving recognition of the overseas programme has affected at least four Malaysian students at the university, leaving them in limbo over mandatory examinations, practical training and their future careers as pharmacists in Malaysia.
The students claimed they only discovered that the programme offered through the ministry is not recognised in Malaysia after nearly four years of study.
The board's Pharmacy Services Programme Division said the pharmacy programmes from Alexandria University currently recognised and listed under the First Schedule of the Registration of Pharmacists Act 1951 (ROPA 1951) are the Bachelor of Pharmaceutical Science and Bachelor of Pharmacy (Clinical Pharmacy).
"However, PBM understands that both programmes were discontinued in 2019.
"At present, Alexandria University offers new pharmacy programmes, namely the Bachelor of Pharmacy - PharmD and Bachelor of Pharmacy - PharmD (Clinical Pharmacy).
"To date, no application has been submitted for the recognition of these new programmes, and therefore they have yet to be recognised by PBM," it said.
When asked about the fate of affected students who are ineligible to sit for the mandatory examinations required to practise as pharmacists in Malaysia, the division said it could not comment further as it was not consulted during the programme offering process by the ministry.
Elaborating further, the division said any new pharmacy programme or changes made to existing recognised programmes, including changes involving the programme name, qualification or university, must undergo a formal application and evaluation process before recognition can be granted by PBM.
"Recognition is only granted after the board is satisfied that the programme's structure, content, teaching and learning methods, infrastructure, and assessment system are comparable to pharmacy programmes recognised in Malaysia.
"The process usually involves a detailed review of submitted documentation, followed by an evaluation visit to the university by an assessment panel appointed by the Malaysian Qualifications Agency and PBM.
"This requirement applies to all universities, both local and foreign, to ensure the programmes are reviewed and assessed according to Malaysian standards in terms of curriculum and training for future graduates.
"As both the Bachelor of Pharmacy - PharmD and Bachelor of Pharmacy - PharmD (Clinical Pharmacy) are new programmes that have never been assessed by PBM, Alexandria University must submit a formal application to PBM for recognition purposes," it said.
The division added that Section 6(1)(a) of the Registration of Pharmacists Act 1951 stipulates that a person may only be registered conditionally if they possess qualifications listed in the First Schedule, before becoming eligible for full registration after completing one year of training at designated government or private facilities.
"As such, graduates from universities not recognised by PBM, namely those not listed under the First Schedule of the Registration of Pharmacists Act 1951, will not be registered as pharmacists in Malaysia.
"This consequently means they are unable to practise as pharmacists in Malaysia," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US imposes fresh sanctions on Iran's military oil sales, Treasury says
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States (US) said on Thursday it has imposed new sanctions on Iran's military oil trade, even as Washington and Tehran reached a tentative agreement to extend their ceasefire and lift restrictions on shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
The Treasury Department said it had sanctioned eight vessels involved in transporting Iranian crude oil and petroleum products to global markets.
The vessels included the Marshall Islands-flagged oil tanker Flora, the Comoros-flagged crude oil tanker Hauncayo and the Panama-flagged tanker Ill Gap.
"We will not allow the Iranian government to increase its oil revenue for the purpose of reconstituting its armed forces and military capabilities," Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a release.
President Donald Trump has yet to approve the ceasefire deal on the war, which the US and Israel launched on Feb 28.
The conflict has roiled global markets by closing the vital strait off Iran and Oman through which 20 per cent of the world's oil and gas has normally flowed.
The US also imposed sanctions on more than 15 entities including Worth Seen Energy Limited in Hong Kong, Symphony Shipping and Maritime Management Inc in Dubai, and Mehdiyev Trading Co, also in Hong Kong.
The Treasury Department said some of the sanctioned Iranian entities also use the oil sales infrastructure of the Iranian armed forces to secure oil products from outside Iran.
It said Worth Seen, for example, procures refined petroleum products for the National Iranian Oil Company on behalf of Sepehr Energy Jahan, the oil sales arm of Iran's Armed Forces General Staff, which the US has previously hit with sanctions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elderly woman missing for three days found safe in oil palm estate
Article:
PONTIAN: Elderly woman Nathiah Selamat, 77, who went missing for three days in Kampung Seri Bunian here, was found safe at 10.50am yesterday.
Nathiah was found by villagers within a 2.4km radius from the location where she was first reported missing.
Villager Mohamed Suib Kamso, 50, who works at an oil palm plantation in Parit Wakaf, Kampung Tenggayon, said he noticed an unusual sound nearby.
He said he later came across an elderly woman who turned out to be the missing person being searched for.
According to him, after finding Nathiah, he immediately ran to inform the search and rescue (SAR) team, which was operating not far from the location.
"The elderly woman appeared confused. After being given drinking water, she claimed she had been walking continuously with a small child until she became tired and fell asleep.
"The SAR team later took Nathiah to Pontian Hospital for treatment," he said.
Mohamed Suib said the elderly woman allegedly followed a small child believed to be a supernatural being through three oil palm plantation areas.
"She started walking through the oil palm plantation in Kampung Medan Sari, then Kampung Seri Bunian, before finally being found in the oil palm plantation area in Kampung Parit Wakaf, Tenggayon," he said.
Earlier, it was reported that the victim went missing on May 26 while believed to be on her way home to Kampung Seri Bunian at 11am.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KPMG Australia says CEO and audit head quit over whistleblower investigation
Article:
SYDNEY: Accounting firm KPMG Australia said on Friday its chief executive officer and head of audit quit after an investigation into complaints raised by a whistleblower about sharing client data fell short of its standards, in a blow to the country's professional services sector.
Chief executive officer Andrew Yates, who joined the firm in 1990 and became chief executive officer in 2021, resigned after its investigation into the whistleblower's allegations "fell short of the firm's expectations, those of the whistleblower and the broader community", the firm said in a statement.
The firm's managing partner of audit and assurance, Julian McPherson, also stepped down and would leave the firm after an orderly transition, the statement added.
"It is clear that in this case we have let ourselves down and I take accountability," Yates said in the same statement.
McPherson said: "Matters have arisen for which I am responsible, and I take accountability."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Inflation pressures building in New Zealand even if Iran war ends soon
Article:
WELLINGTON: Inflation pressures are building in New Zealand even if the Middle East conflict ends soon, a senior central banker said on Friday, adding that she would be looking at high-frequency data to see if a July rate hike would be warranted.
Karen Silk, an assistant governor at the Reserve Bank of New Zealand who voted to keep policy steady this week, told Reuters that the central bank needed to be forward looking and did not necessarily have to wait for the quarterly CPI print, due after the July meeting, to move.
The RBNZ held the official cash rate (OCR) at 2.25 per cent on Wednesday, after Governor Anna Breman used her casting vote to break a 3-3 split. Three external members voted for a rate hike.
Silk said she saw inflation pressures building in the New Zealand economy due to higher oil prices, but there was some real uncertainty around the duration of the Middle East conflict. Still, some damage has been done even if the war ends soon, she added.
Sharp declines in business sentiment and household confidence, significant spare capacity in the economy, recent tightening in financial conditions, still-low core inflation and wage growth were among reasons she voted to hold interest rates steady on Wednesday.
"I do hold the perspective that we can't have the oil price shock that we've had without it having some flow through further down the line. And so therefore, you do need to have an increasing OCR to lean against that," said Silk.
Silk said between now and July, the central bank would get some more high-frequency data and have an idea of where the conflict is headed.
When asked if September would be a more opportune time to raise interest rates with the release of the quarterly inflation print, Silk said: "We cannot just look at data that talks to us about history. We have always got to be forward looking."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens higher against US dollar on improved sentiment
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  The ringgit opened higher against the greenback on Friday amid news about the possible United States-Iran truce, which is expected to have a positive impact on the local note, said an analyst.
At 8am, the local currency rose to 3.9755/9815 against the greenback from Thursday's close of 3.9770/9805.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid expects the ringgit-US dollar pair to trade in a RM3.95 to RM3.97 range today.
"News on the 60-day US-Iran truce has led to a decline in Brent crude oil prices by 1.04 per cent to US$93.31 per barrel. The news (about the truce) is unconfirmed, but the market has reacted positively.
"This suggests that the investing community is fatigued with the nature of the unstable negotiations," he told Bernama.
At the same time, the US personal consumption expenditures (PCE) inflation rate accelerated to 3.8 per cent in April from 3.5 per cent the month before, indicating that higher oil prices have eroded Americans' purchasing power.
Meanwhile, initial jobless claims also climbed to 215,000 last week from 210,000 previously. It has been on the uptrend since April 30.
As a result, the US Dollar Index (DXY) was down by 0.21 per cent to 98.997 points.
Back home, the ringgit traded lower against a basket of major currencies.
It fell against the British pound to 5.3447/3527 from 5.3272/3319 at Thursday's close, declined against the euro to 4.6323/6392 from 4.6181/6222, and eased versus the Japanese yen to 2.4961/5000 from yesterday's 2.4947/4970.
The local note slid versus the Singapore dollar to 3.1151/1201 from 3.1085/1115 at Thursday's close, and slipped against the Thai baht to 12.2083/2338  from 12.1613/1773 previously.
It was little changed against the Indonesian rupiah at 222.7/223.2 versus 222.8/223.1 previously and was flat vis-a-vis the the Philippine peso at 6.45/6.46.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mitsui seeks LNG investments to meet power demand from data centres
Article:
TOKYO: Japanese trading house Mitsui is looking to invest in LNG projects across the Middle East, the US and Australia to meet rising power demand from data centres, Bloomberg News reported on Thursday, citing its chief executive officer Kenichi Hori.
The company will consider taking equity stakes or securing supply agreements in LNG and gas chemicals firms, Hori told Bloomberg News, adding that demand for LNG was booming as companies sought clean energy to power AI infrastructure.
Mitsui did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
The company, in which Berkshire Hathaway owns a 10 per cent stake, is one of Japan's top five trading houses and has a portfolio spanning fossil fuel production and trading, renewable energy and food.
Hori said earlier this month that Mitsui wanted to "capture upside opportunities in the energy sector while remaining cautious".
Japan, one of the world's most vulnerable countries to energy import disruptions, has intensified diplomatic efforts and pledged billions of yen in support to cushion the economic shock from the Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
Mitsui already has an interest in Abu Dhabi National Oil Co's Ruwais liquefied natural gas (LNG) export facility and will consider further investment in the Middle East, Hori told Bloomberg News.
Last year, the company signed a long-term agreement with Venture Global to supply 1 million metric tonnes per annum of LNG.
Mitsui also partners with Woodside in Australia's North West Shelf project, the country's oldest and second-largest LNG plant.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Costco gas stations draw more frugal Americans as prices bite
Article:
NEW YORK: Costco Wholesale reaped the spoils of surging gas prices, as more consumers sought out its lower-priced fuel, helping it top market expectations for third-quarter US sales.
Just as Walmart beat sales expectations last week while warning that consumer spending remains stressed from inflation, Costco painted a picture of a budget-conscious shopper driven to its members-only wholesale club as price anxiety rises.
The final five weeks of the quarter were Costco's highest ever in terms of sales volume, chief executive officer Ron Vachris said on an earnings conference call on Thursday.
Gas prices exceeding US$4 per gallon, on the rise as the Iran war drags on, drove many Costco members to use its gas stations for the first time, Vachris said.
The company took advantage, lowering gas prices to drive volume. "We've widened our gaps in terms of price," chief financial officer Gary Millerchip said on the call.
"We know that's something that's very high on our members' minds."
The uptick in fuel fill-ups could boost Costco's bottom line, as members who use its gas stations typically spend more in the warehouses, Vachris said.
FOOD PRICES LOWER THAN OTHER CATEGORIES
Costco said it has maintained lower prices on beef, which, along with higher transportation costs, affected its margin in the third quarter.
Prices, however, stayed higher for appliances due to the elevated cost of memory chips, Millerchip said.
If oil prices stay high, items with plastic, or apparel made of polyester or cotton, could remain more expensive, he said.
"That investment in everyday-low-price positioning during a period of macroeconomic uncertainty is classic Costco," said Bryan Hayes, stock strategist at Zacks Investment Research.
But, he said, "it does suggest that the gross margin line could face modest near-term pressure as the company prioritises member loyalty over rate."
Including gas, US comparable sales jumped 9.4 per cent during the quarter, beating analysts' estimate of a near 7 per cent rise, according to data compiled by LSEG.
Costco's shares have gained about 15 per cent so far this year, compared with an 8 per cent rise in the S&P 500 Consumer Staples index. They were unchanged in extended trading.
Costco is also investing in fast delivery, at a time when giants such as Amazon and Walmart are whittling down shipping times in a quest for e-commerce dominance.
Costco's average same-day delivery time in the US is now less than 45 minutes, Vachris said.
Refunds from outlawed tariffs, meanwhile, were expected to begin flowing in the next two to three months, the company said.
Vachris reiterated that tariff refunds would pass on to consumers in some form, depending on the amount of return and developments in the March class action lawsuit filed against Costco by customers seeking a share of refunds.
Total quarterly revenue rose 12 per cent to US$70.53 billion, beating analysts' estimate of US$69.81 billion.
Its net income jumped 15 per cent to US$2.19 billion, while adjusted earnings per share of US$4.93 met expectations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US, Iran near deal but Trump sign-off awaited
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States and Iran closed in on a framework for a ceasefire extension detail Thursday but President Donald Trump has yet to approve any agreement, US officials said.
US sources told AFP earlier that the two sides had reached agreement on a memorandum of understanding (MOU) to prolong the truce for 60 days.
But US Vice President JD Vance said Trump -- who was notably silent throughout the day -- had yet to sign on the dotted line. There was no immediate reaction from Iran.
"It's hard to say exactly when or if the President is going to sign the MOU," Vance, who has played a key role in the negotiations, told reporters.
"We're going back and forth on a couple of language points. We've made a lot of progress here."
Vance said the Iranians were negotiating in "at least so far in good faith" and both sides wanted to reopen the Straits of Hormuz -- but there was still disagreement on Iran's stockpile of enriched uranium.
"Hopefully, we'll continue to make progress and the president will be in a position where he can endorse the agreement, but obviously that's still TBD," meaning to be determined, he added.
US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent earlier said that "we perhaps have the makings of a deal here" but said some of Trump's red lines had still not been met.
"He's not going to take a bad deal. He's going to make a great deal for the American people," Bessent said in a briefing at the White House.
'NOT SATISFIED'
US sources had earlier confirmed a report by the Axios news outlet that the MOU would open the Strait of Hormuz but leave Iran's nuclear programme unresolved, with further talks to follow on the issue.
Axios reported that the 60-day deal will say that shipping through the Strait of Hormuz will be unrestricted, with no tolls or harassment and with Iran having to remove all mines within 30 days.
The US will in return lift its naval blockade on Iran's ports, but only in proportion to how much commercial shipping is restored, it said.
The memorandum will also contain an Iranian commitment not to pursue a nuclear weapon, Axios said. Among the first issues to be addressed is how to dispose of Iran's stocks of enriched uranium.
Trump said during a cabinet meeting on Wednesday that he was "not satisfied" yet with Iran's offers and warned that he could "finish the job" militarily.
Trump has repeatedly insisted that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon under any deal to end the war that the US and Israel launched on Feb 28. A fragile ceasefire has been in place since April 7.
Washington and Teheran both accused each other of violating the truce earlier on Thursday following an exchange of fire.
But Treasury chief Bessent insisted the ceasefire remained in effect.
"President Trump always prefers a peace deal, so everything we have done thus far has been defensive, and at present that's what we'll continue doing," he added. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil prices mixed amid conflicting US-Iran ceasefire deal reports
Article:
NEW YORK: Oil prices settled mixed on Thursday after a choppy trading session, as traders mulled conflicting reports of progress on a potential deal to extend a ceasefire between the US and Iran.
Brent crude futures for July, which expire on Friday's settlement, closed down 58 cents, or 0.6 per cent, at US$93.71 a barrel.
The more actively traded August Brent futures were last trading up by 72 cents at US$92.97 as of 3:20 p.m. EDT (1720 GMT).
US oil futures eked out marginal gains to settle up 22 cents, or 0.3 per cent, at US$88.90 a barrel.
Oil prices have been volatile in recent sessions on conflicting signals on the possibility of an end to the three-month Iran war and potential re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz.
Traffic through the maritime chokepoint remains a small fraction of the pre-war level.
An agreement had been reached to extend the ceasefire in the Middle East for 60 days, four sources familiar with the matter told Reuters. News outlet Axios first reported about the deal on Thursday.
The agreement still needs US President Donald Trump's approval, sources told Reuters. Iran's Tasnim news agency, meanwhile, said the text of a potential memorandum of understanding with the US has not yet been finalised or confirmed.
In early trading, Brent and WTI futures were up more than two per cent, after Iran's Revolutionary Guards said they had targeted a US air base in response to a US attack on the port city of Bandar Abbas.
"The complex continues to advance grudgingly on bullish developments out of Iran while plunging markedly on even the slightest suggestion of a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz," oil trading advisory firm Ritterbusch and Associates said.
"This contrast in responses to bullish and bearish inputs could continue as long as the ceasefire remains intact."
Oil prices were also under pressure from official US data that showed the country's crude oil stockpiles fell by 3.3 million barrels last week, a sixth consecutive week of declines but lower than the 4.1-million-barrel draw analysts polled by Reuters expected.
US gasoline and distillate fuel stockpiles also fell.
The oil market remains more sensitive to Middle East headlines despite another week of large declines in US stockpiles, UBS analyst Giovanni Staunovo said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: S&P 500, Nasdaq hit record highs on extended US-Iran ceasefire
Article:
NEW YORK: The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq posted record closing highs on Thursday after news reports said the US and Iran had reached a draft agreement to extend their ceasefire for 60 days, while investors also digested key inflation data.
The Dow Jones Industrial Average, which was subdued during the session, eked out small gains in afternoon trade to finish at another closing high.
The agreement still needs US President Donald Trump's approval, sources told Reuters. Iran's Tasnim news agency, meanwhile, said the text of a potential memorandum of understanding with the US has not yet been finalised or confirmed.
"Traders are on a hair trigger with the back-and-forth on deal news, and have been leaning long to avoid getting trampled by a better-than-expected outcome. The harder part is that the inflationary forces may not abate as fast as markets want," said Jamie Cox, managing partner at Harris Financial Group.
Economic data showed US inflation increased at its fastest pace in three years in April, driven by higher energy prices amid the Iran war.
Meanwhile, US GDP for the first quarter was revised lower to a 1.6 per cent annualised increase, with momentum expected to slow this quarter.
The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 24.69 points, or 0.05 per cent, to 50,668.97, the S&P 500 gained 43.27 points, or 0.58 per cent, to 7,563.63 and the Nasdaq Composite gained 242.74 points, or 0.91 per cent, to 26,917.47.
The S&P 500 healthcare index posted strong gains. Eli Lilly advanced four per cent after CVS Health said it would restore the drugmaker's weight-loss injection, Zepbound, to its coverage and add its newly approved obesity pill Foundayo.
Tech shares also moved higher. Microsoft gained 3.5 per cent after news website The Information reported that the company would release a new coding model next week.
Marvell Technology rose three per cent after UBS raised its target price to US$230 from US$195.
The company's shares have more than doubled so far this year.
Snowflake shares soared 36 per cent after the data analytics firm lifted its annual product revenue forecast and announced a five-year AI infrastructure deal worth US$6 billion with Amazon Web Services.
Peers Datadog and MongoDB also climbed.
Renewed confidence in AI and earnings growth momentum have underscored the recent rally despite the Middle East tensions, which have increased inflationary expectations.
"Markets continue to look through these risks because the global economy and corporate earnings remain relatively resilient," said Jitania Kandhari, deputy CIO, solutions and multi-assets, at Morgan Stanley Investment Management.
"Geopolitical instability could ultimately accelerate spending in areas tied to AI, including cybersecurity, defence technology, energy infrastructure and supply-chain resiliency, reinforcing the long-term investment case."
While the S&P 500 is trading at roughly 21 to 22 times forward earnings versus a trailing 10-year average of 19.7 times, investors are less concerned because earnings expectations are rising faster than stock prices, Kandhari said.
Among other movers, Dollar Tree climbed almost 18 per cent after the discount retailer lifted its full-year profit forecast, while Best Buy also rose 15.8 per cent after the electronics vendor forecast second-quarter sales above estimates.
Drone companies rose after The Wall Street Journal reported that the Trump administration was in talks to fund drone firms. Shares of Unusual Machines surged almost 11 per cent.
Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 1.74-to-one ratio on the New York Stock Exchange. There were 526 new highs and 99 new lows on the NYSE.
On the Nasdaq, 3,114 stocks rose and 1,728 fell as advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 1.8-to-one ratio.
The S&P 500 posted 18 new 52-week highs and 10 new lows while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 127 new highs and 65 new lows.
Volume on US exchanges was 19.2 billion shares, compared with the 19.03 billion average for the full session over the last 20 trading days.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US dollar weakens on reports of US-Iran ceasefire deal
Article:
NEW YORK: The US dollar fell against major currencies on Thursday after reports the US and Iran had reached an agreement to extend a ceasefire, though several similar reports over the course of the three-month conflict have not resulted in an end to the war.
The dollar has swung in recent weeks, in line with the shifting outlook on the Middle East conflict, gaining when markets expect a prolonged stand-off and falling when reports signal a move towards de-escalation.
According to four sources familiar with the matter, the agreement would extend the truce for another 60 days and allow traffic to flow through the strategic waterway while negotiators tackle difficult issues such as Iran's nuclear programme.
Axios was the first to report the agreement.
The euro gained 0.2 per cent against the dollar to US$1.1649. Against the Swiss franc, the dollar weakened 0.37 per cent to 0.784.
The dollar index, which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, fell 0.3 per cent to 99, putting it on track to snap two sessions of gains following the resumption of hostilities between the US and Iran.
US equity markets gained on the day with the Nasdaq adding nearly one per cent. US Treasury bond yields fell. The yield on benchmark US 10-year notes fell 2.8 basis points to 4.453 per cent.
SOFT US DATA
US inflation increased at its fastest pace in three years in April, driven by higher energy prices amid the war with Iran, signalling that the Federal Reserve could hold interest rates unchanged for longer.
The personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index rose 0.4 per cent month-on-month in April after shooting up 0.7 per cent in March.
However, core PCE inflation, which excludes food and energy prices, gained 0.2 per cent in April on a monthly basis after advancing 0.3 per cent in March. US economic growth for the first quarter was also revised lower.
"The combination of core PCE, which came in a little bit softer, and the growth data also coming in a little softer sends a message that perhaps that the Fed can be a little bit less aggressive with its higher-for-longer, which is somewhat risk-supportive," said Joel Kruger, market strategist at London-based LMAX Group.
"Ultimately, until we figure out a resolution to the geopolitical side and what it's going to be with inflation, it's a lot of kind of just choppy and directionless trade."
ON WATCH FOR YEN INTERVENTION
Investors are eyeing whether Japanese officials would step in again to support the yen as it trades near the 160-per-dollar psychological level.
The Japanese yen was up 0.19 per cent against the greenback to 159.22 per dollar.
The risk-sensitive Australian dollar was up 0.32 per cent to US$0.71645.
The Aussie dollar remains the top-performing G10 currency versus the US dollar this year, rising 7.35 per cent year-to-date.
The New Zealand dollar rose 0.51 per cent to US$0.59315, adding to gains from the prior session after the Reserve Bank of New Zealand signalled a hawkish shift.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Leadership effectiveness without micromanaging staff
Article:
Leadership is often misunderstood as the constant supervision of people and processes. Many managers believe that closely monitoring every task ensures quality, efficiency, and accountability.
However, excessive control can create frustration, reduce confidence, and weaken the very performance leaders are trying to improve. Effective leadership is not about controlling every decision. It is about creating an environment where people feel trusted, motivated, and capable of performing at their best.
Micromanagement often develops from fear. Some leaders fear mistakes, missed deadlines, or a loss of authority. Others believe that nobody can complete a task as well as they can. While these concerns may come from a desire for excellence, the outcome is usually damaging. Employees who are constantly checked on begin to lose confidence in their own judgement. They may stop showing initiative because they expect every decision to be questioned or corrected. Over time, this weakens morale and limits creativity.
An effective leader understands that trust is essential. Trust does not mean abandoning responsibility or ignoring performance. It means allowing people the freedom to complete their work while still providing support when necessary. Employees who feel trusted are usually more engaged and committed. They become more willing to solve problems independently and take ownership of their responsibilities. This sense of ownership often leads to stronger performance and greater job satisfaction.
Communication plays a central role in leadership without micromanagement. Staff members need clear expectations, realistic goals, and a proper understanding of their responsibilities. Problems often arise when leaders provide vague instructions and later become frustrated when outcomes differ from expectations. Effective leaders avoid this by communicating clearly from the beginning. Once expectations are understood, employees should be allowed the space to work in their own way.
Strong leaders focus on outcomes rather than controlling every step of the process. Different people approach tasks differently. One employee may prefer detailed planning, while another may work more effectively through flexibility and adaptation. As long as standards are met and objectives are achieved, leaders should avoid unnecessary interference. Allowing employees to use their own strengths encourages confidence and innovation within the workplace.
Delegation is another important quality of effective leadership. Delegating responsibilities demonstrates confidence in employees and allows leaders to focus on wider organisational goals. However, delegation only succeeds when leaders genuinely release control. Some managers assign tasks but continue demanding constant updates or repeatedly altering instructions. This creates confusion and frustration. Proper delegation means providing guidance, setting expectations, and then trusting employees to complete the work.
Supportive leadership is far more productive than controlling leadership. Employees perform better when they know their leader is available to assist rather than criticise. Leaders should create an atmosphere where staff feel comfortable asking questions, discussing concerns, and admitting mistakes. Fear-based environments encourage people to hide problems rather than solve them. A supportive culture promotes honesty, learning, and improvement.
Feedback is also essential in leadership. Effective leaders provide constructive feedback without making employees feel incapable. Instead of focusing only on errors, strong leaders recognise achievements and encourage development. Balanced feedback improves confidence and helps employees grow professionally. When people feel valued and respected, they are more likely to remain motivated and loyal.
Micromanagement can also damage productivity. Constant interruptions, excessive meetings, and repeated checking slow down work considerably. Employees may spend more time reporting progress than completing tasks. In contrast, leaders who trust their teams often create more efficient workplaces. Staff members can focus on meaningful work rather than satisfying unnecessary supervision.
Another important aspect of leadership effectiveness is emotional intelligence. Leaders who understand human behaviour are better equipped to motivate people positively. Emotional intelligence involves listening carefully, recognising emotions, and responding appropriately to different situations. Employees are individuals with different personalities, strengths, and pressures. Leaders who treat staff with empathy and understanding often gain greater respect and cooperation.
Confidence is a defining trait of effective leadership. Insecure leaders may micromanage because they fear losing control or appearing weak. Confident leaders understand that empowering others does not reduce their authority. In fact, successful teams often reflect strong leadership. Leadership should be measured not by how dependent staff are on the manager, but by how confidently they can perform their duties.
Ultimately, leadership effectiveness is not about controlling every detail. It is about guiding people towards success while allowing them the dignity and confidence to contribute meaningfully. The best leaders understand that employees perform at their highest level when they feel trusted, respected, and supported. Rather than creating dependence through micromanagement, effective leaders build strong, capable teams that can operate with confidence and independence.
A successful leader is, therefore, not someone who constantly watches over employees but someone who inspires them to perform well even when no supervision is present. That is the true measure of leadership effectiveness.
* The writer is managing consultant and executive leadership coach at EQTD Consulting. He is also the author of the national bestseller "So, You Want To Get Promoted?"
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Petronas Suriname Block 52 may lift Malaysia's energy role [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Petroliam Nasional Bhd's (Petronas) oil exploration activities at offshore Block 52 in Suriname could position Malaysia within a globally significant emerging energy corridor, strengthening its role in the international upstream oil and gas sector.
According to a Reuters report on Thursday, the national oil company is exploring for oil at the block, which was declared commercially viable for natural gas development last November following major discoveries in the area, said.
Economist Samirul Ariff Othman told Business Times that developments at Suriname's offshore Block 52 are strategically important for Petronas for few reasons.
"The basin is gradually evolving into one of the most promising new offshore hydrocarbon provinces outside the Middle East. The neighbouring Guyana–Suriname basin has already attracted major global players such as ExxonMobil, TotalEnergies and APA Corporation because of repeated large offshore discoveries," he said.
Samirul said the block could play a significant role in Petronas' international upstream strategy, particularly in terms of geographic diversification.
He noted that Malaysia's mature upstream basins, especially offshore Peninsular Malaysia and parts of Sabah and Sarawak, face natural reserve depletion over time.
"Petronas therefore requires new long-cycle international assets to sustain reserve replacement ratios and long-term production resilience.
"Suriname gives Petronas exposure to: a new hydrocarbon basin, deepwater growth opportunities, and potentially lower-contaminant crude and gas resources.
"This aligns with the company's broader strategy of maintaining a geographically diversified upstream portfolio across Latin America, Africa and Asia," he said.
Samirul said the company's strategic presence in the Guyana–Suriname basin is also significant, as it now holds interests in multiple offshore Suriname blocks, including Blocks 48, 52, 53, 63, 64 and 66.
"That suggests Petronas is not treating Suriname as a short-term speculative venture. It appears to be building a long-duration regional upstream position," he said.
He said that if Petronas moves towards large-scale development in Suriname, Malaysian oil and gas service providers could benefit across a range of areas
This includes engineering services, subsea support, offshore maintenance, drilling services, logistics, fabrication, digital monitoring systems and floating liquefied natural gas (FLNG) related expertise.
"Malaysia possesses a relatively mature offshore ecosystem built around: MISC Bhd, Vantris Energy Bhd, Yinson Holdings Bhd, Dialog Group Bhd, and numerous small and medium enterprises vendors within the Petronas ecosystem.
"Particularly important is Malaysia's accumulated expertise in: deepwater operations, offshore engineering, and FLNG systems," he said.
Samirul said that such technical expertise is not easily replicated by newer producers.
However, he noted that the extent of Malaysian participation would depend on factors such as local content requirements in Suriname, international consortium structures, project economics, and global contractor competition.
"Still, if Block 52 moves toward full development, this could create opportunities for Malaysia's broader oil-and-gas ecosystem, not just Petronas itself.
"In strategic terms, Suriname may eventually represent more than a single overseas asset. It could become part of Petronas' next-generation international upstream architecture: LNG-linked, deepwater-oriented, geographically diversified, and integrated into emerging Atlantic Basin energy flows," Samirul said.
OCUIo10En0o
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: FMM urges tax relief as supply chain disruptions hit manufacturers [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Federation of Malaysian Manufacturers (FMM) has called on the Finance Ministry to temporarily suspend import duties and sales taxes on critical raw materials, as manufacturers reroute sourcing away from disrupted Middle Eastern supply chains towards alternative markets including China, India and Asean countries.
FMM president Jacob Lee Chor Kok told Business Times that the industry body has also requested a double tax deduction on extraordinary logistics costs, saying these unplanned expenses are weighing heavily on manufacturers' working capital amid ongoing supply chain disruptions.
He added that FMM has urged the government to temporarily expand diesel subsidy quotas for industrial freight and domestic haulage operators, warning that worsening logistics bottlenecks could threaten manufacturing continuity in the second half of 2026.
Lee said transport bottlenecks have intensified as haulage operators exhaust subsidised diesel allocations earlier than expected, driven by longer port waiting times and rerouted supply chains linked to the prolonged Middle East conflict.
He warned that the conflict is emerging as a serious risk to industrial operations, with disruptions potentially shifting from a logistics issue into a broader structural threat involving production slowdowns and temporary shutdowns.
According to FMM's latest impact surveys, disruptions to global shipping corridors and energy markets have pushed raw material shortages to critical levels, particularly key inputs such as resins, polymers, industrial chemicals and metals.
"Already, 70 per cent of manufacturers have reported severely deteriorating supply conditions. This is compounded by soaring freight costs, domestic haulage bottlenecks arising from diesel quota exhaustion, and severe working capital strain affecting 68 per cent of firms," he said.
Lee said FMM has also found that nearly half of Malaysian manufacturers, or 48.2 per cent, have been forced to reduce output or suspend certain product lines.
"If targeted fiscal relief measures, tax exemptions on inputs and diesel subsidy expansions are not implemented swiftly, the current situation of reduced shift hours and deferred investments affecting 60 per cent of manufacturers could escalate into factory shutdowns and widespread retrenchments," he said.
Lee said downstream plastics fabricators, chemical manufacturers and industrial processors face the highest risk of prolonged shutdowns, citing a tightening of West Asian petrochemical feedstocks such as naphtha, benzene, liquefied petroleum gas and sulphur.
He added that electrical and electronics (E&E) manufacturers are also highly exposed due to depleted inventories and longer lead times for speciality chemicals used in surface treatments and industrial coatings.
"FMM also cautions that heat-intensive and high-diesel-intensity sectors such as ceramics, quarrying, glass manufacturing and heat-intensive food processing are on the brink of operational suspension.
"These energy-reliant industries are struggling to survive under a combination of high global oil prices and fuel pressures. Since margins are thin, companies are unable to absorb sustained production cost increases exceeding 10 per cent," he said.
Lee also said FMM's May 2026 impact survey shows manufacturing small and medium enterprises (SMEs) are bearing the brunt of the crisis due to narrower cost bases and limited financial buffers.
He said 68 per cent of smaller firms are under severe cash flow pressure, exacerbated by a double squeeze where suppliers demand advance payments while buyers delay payments due to extended shipping transit times.
"To manage day-to-day operations under these conditions, SMEs are aggressively sourcing alternative raw materials from regional neighbours such as China and Thailand, despite facing quality mismatches and lengthy client approval timelines," he said.
He added that firms are deferring long-term capital investments, tapering production volumes and introducing flexible or hybrid work structures to mitigate rising overheads and preserve working capital.
Factory Closures Reflect Rising 'High Demand, Low Supply Resilience' Risk in 2026
UniKL Business School economic analyst Associate Professor Dr Aimi Zulhazmi Abdul Rashid said temporary factory closures under current conditions reflect a "high demand, low supply resilience" risk that has become more pronounced in 2026.
He said even short shutdowns of three to seven days can lead to permanent export losses as global buyers quickly shift orders to competing manufacturing hubs such as Vietnam, Thailand and Indonesia.
"In many cases, contracts are not recovered for two to three years due to loss of supply confidence," he said.
He added that fixed operating costs continue even during shutdowns, with wages, rent, loans and maintenance still payable.
"A one-week closure can erode about 1.5 to two per cent of margins. Many E&E and furniture players are already operating on margins of around six to eight per cent," he said.
Aimi said disruptions at a single plant, particularly in Penang, can ripple through global supply chains, affecting downstream assembly lines in markets such as Germany as buyers shift to perceived more stable suppliers.
On external pressures, he said prolonged Middle East tensions and potential disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz would continue to push global energy costs higher.
Malaysia, while a net exporter of liquefied natural gas, remains a net importer of crude oil, diesel and refined fuels, resulting in a net negative impact from rising energy prices, he said.
He added that inflationary pressure is already filtering through the economy via fuel and transport costs, with Brent crude at about US$98 per barrel pushing RON97 to RM4.85 per litre and diesel to RM4.97 per litre in late May 2026.
"As diesel accounts for around 80 per cent of goods transport, every 10 sen increase adds about 0.15 to 0.2 per cent to CPI for food and household items," he said.
He added that higher freight insurance premiums for Gulf routes have also raised landed costs by two to four per cent.
If diesel remains above RM4.80 for more than two months, second-round effects such as wage adjustments and service price increases are likely, he said.
Aimi said logistics, airlines, food processing and construction sectors are among the most exposed.
He noted that airlines such as AirAsia X have flagged jet fuel cost increases of about RM90 million a month at current oil prices.
He added that SMEs in food and beverage and construction are likely to freeze hiring or reduce shifts amid margin pressure.
To improve resilience, Aimi suggested diversification of sourcing beyond China to include Thailand, Indonesia and Turkey, alongside forward buying of raw materials to hedge against oil price volatility.
He also recommended that exporters include price adjustment clauses tied to Brent crude movements above US$100 per barrel, a mechanism already used in some European contracts.
IPP Wealth Managers director of investment strategy and chief economist Mohd Sedek Jantan said the concerns raised by FMM are valid given the fragility of global supply chains and Malaysia's reliance on imported inputs and external demand.
He noted that Malaysia's manufacturing sales rose 5.3 per cent year-on-year to RM173.1 billion in March 2026, while employment growth expanded only 1.1 per cent, suggesting limited slack in the sector.
"Temporary shutdowns within the manufacturing sector could have a disproportionately larger impact on Malaysia's growth outlook because manufacturing remains one of the key pillars of the economy, particularly in exports, industrial production, employment and SME activity.
"The immediate impact would likely appear through slower industrial output, weaker export growth and softer private sector activity.
"However, the broader concern is the spillover effect across the domestic economic ecosystem," he said.
Sedek said policy support should shift towards long-term resilience, including supplier diversification, automation, digitalisation, and strengthening domestic vendor ecosystems.
He also called for improved logistics efficiency through faster customs clearance, better port operations and infrastructure upgrades.
"Beyond immediate support measures, Malaysia also needs to strengthen its medium-term competitiveness.
"In a more fragmented global environment, countries that can offer operational stability, reliable supply chains and resilient industrial ecosystems will likely attract a larger share of global trade and investment flows.
"Ultimately, the issue is no longer solely about managing short-term geopolitical shocks.
"It is increasingly about whether Malaysia can position its manufacturing sector to remain resilient, competitive and integrated within evolving global supply chains over the next decade," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Austrian jihadist jailed for 15 years for Taylor Swift concert attack plan
Article:
WIENER NEUSTADT, Austria: An Austrian court on Thursday sentenced a 21-year-old who admitted plotting a foiled jihadist attack on a Taylor Swift concert to 15 years in jail.
The US megastar was forced to cancel the three Vienna shows of her record-breaking "Eras" tour in the summer of 2024 after authorities warned of the plot by the so-called Islamic State group (IS).
Accused of planning the attack and forming an IS cell, the jury found Austrian national Beran A. guilty on all counts after deliberating for hours.
The judge then handed him a 15-year jail sentence.
While listening to the verdict, Beran A. looked into the court room repeatedly, sniffling loudly, his leg and hands shaking.
Beran A. went on trial last month on terror offences and other charges in Wiener Neustadt outside Vienna.
The defendant – who was arrested the day before the cancelled concert and has been detained since – pleaded guilty to all charges except being an accomplice to attempted murder.
BOMB-MAKING INSTRUCTIONS
Beran A. faced a maximum sentence of up to 20 years in prison.
Another 21-year-old, Arda K., who was on trial together with Beran A., was sentenced to 12 years in jail.
The sentences can still be appealed.
In their closing remarks, both defendants apologised.
"I just want to say that I'm sorry," Beran A. told the court.
In his testimony last month, Beran A. said he had become convinced that he "had to wage jihad" but was "afraid to die."
He told the court he picked the packed Ernst Happel Stadium in Vienna during Swift's concert as a target.
He detailed how he got instructions and tried but failed to make a bomb. He also sought advice on what weapons to choose in several chat groups and from a high-ranking IS member.
'SEND A CLEAR SIGNAL'
Beran A. and Arda K. were accused, together with a third Austrian, Hasan E., of forming a "highly dangerous IS terror cell" planning to carry out several attacks, mainly abroad, in the name of IS, prosecutors say.
Hasan E. is currently in prison in Saudi Arabia, accused of stabbing a security official in Mecca in 2024 and injuring four others.
Defence lawyer Mair used her closing argument to urge the jury to acquit Beran of the charge of encouraging Hasan over the stabbing, arguing there was no evidence.
"Beran is not a leader, he is not an ideological mastermind," she said.
The prosecution told the jury they had the "chance to send a clear signal" that those who commit crimes will be held accountable on all counts, adding Beran A. encouraged Hasan E. through "intensive contact" and other ways.
Beran A. is accused of having been a member of a terror organisation from 2023, according to prosecutors.
By sharing IS propaganda, he participated and "openly aligned himself" with IS, they said.
The Swift concert plot was thwarted with the help of US intelligence.
Swift later wrote on social media that "the reason for the cancellations filled me with a new sense of fear, and a tremendous amount of guilt because so many had planned on coming to those shows."
Last year, a Berlin court convicted a Syrian teenager of contributing to the plot to attack the Swift concert.
The 16-year-old was given an 18-month suspended sentence.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump's face could appear on US$250 bill
Article:
WASHINGTON: President Donald Trump could soon appear on a new US$250 bill, in the Republican's latest move to shatter US traditions by putting his personal stamp on national institutions.
A proposal for the new bill, featuring a glaring Trump, was first reported Thursday by the Washington Post.
If carried out, it would be the first time the image of a living person -- let alone a president -- had appeared on US currency in a century and a half.
"Right now there is proposed legislation -- front of the House, in front of the Senate -- to change the first requirement so that a living person, Donald J. Trump, could be on a US$250 bill," US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told a media briefing Thursday.
"I don't think that there's anything untoward about having the President of the United States, the person who's president of the United States, on the 250th anniversary bill," Bessent said.
He said the Treasury Department has made advance preparations if legislation were passed, but "will stick to the law."
A design mock-up obtained by the Washington Post showed the words "America 250 anniversary," a nod to the United States declaring its independence on July 4, 1776.
According to the Post, two Trump appointees at the Treasury Department last year began urging staff at the Bureau of Engraving and Printing to prepare prototypes.
Employees there, speaking on condition of anonymity, told the Post that the plan raised concerns because it would violate a federal law banning the depiction of living presidents on US money.
A Treasury spokeswoman told AFP that the Bureau of Engraving and Printing is "conducting appropriate planning and due diligence" in response to the proposal.
She added that US Treasurer Brandon Beach had not asked staff to print the note before congressional action occurred.
Printing bureau director Patricia Solimene had pushed back, warning officials including Beach of legal and procedural obstacles, the employees told the Post.
Solimene was abruptly reassigned from her role, the paper reported.
Such worries have not stopped the Trump administration from pressing ahead with its effort to slap his likeness or name on cultural institutions and other items -- sparking accusations of a cult of personality around the 79-year-old leader.
Earlier this year the US Commission of Fine Arts, whose members were appointed by Trump, unanimously approved the minting of a commemorative "Semiquincentennial Gold Coin" made of 24-carat gold.
In recent months both the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts and the US Institute of Peace have been rebranded to include Trump's name.
His likeness also stares down from banners draping the Department of Justice and Department of Agriculture -- and it will soon appear in some US passports, according to the State Department.
Legislation to allow Trump to appear on a US$250 bill was introduced in Congress last year but has sat idle.
Reaction among Democrats was critical, with Senator Mark Warner, a member of the Senate's banking committee, saying the unprecedented proposal amounted to the White House blatantly "stoking the president's ego." - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Test kits key to sniffing out fentanyl [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Test kits to detect fentanyl are crucial in efforts to combat the extremely potent drug  as   it is  colourless, odourless and easily trafficked due to the small amount needed for thousands of users, said   experts.
Asian Criminological Society board member Datuk Dr P. Sundramoorthy said     even a very small amount of fentanyl could suppress breathing and lead to death within minutes.
He said unlike traditional narcotics, fentanyl could be easily mixed into other drugs, pills or even vape liquids without the users' knowledge, significantly increasing the risk of   overdose.
Sundramoorthy said synthetic drugs such as fentanyl were highly profitable products for organised crime groups.
"Small quantities can produce thousands of doses, making trafficking easier, more discreet and more lucrative.
"Its synthetic nature further reduces reliance on traditional cultivation methods, enabling criminal groups to manufacture and distribute the drug more efficiently through clandestine networks."
Fentanyl is an opioid made entirely in laboratories. According to the United States National Institues of Health, as little as 2mg  of fentanyl can be fatal. By comparison,  a lethal dose of cocaine is about 600 times more.
Sundramoorthy said fentanyl could cause extreme drowsiness, respiratory depression, loss of consciousness and  breathing failure.
This potency is why   fentanyl is often mixed into other substances, like vape liquids.
Sundramoorthy called for stricter regulation of vape liquids  that includes laboratory testing and stronger enforcement to prevent drug rings from using them as a medium of distribution.
He  also warned that  younger vape users might be particularly vulnerable to drug-laced liquids as they  might underestimate the danger of the product.
"Vape products are often perceived as less harmful, which reduces risk awareness.
"If fentanyl is introduced into such products, users may unknowingly consume highly dangerous substances with potentially fatal consequences."
Toxicologist Professor Dr Mustafa Ali Mohd said fentanyl was currently among the most concerning synthetic opioids globally.
He said fentanyl was estimated to be about 50 to 100 times more potent than morphine.
"The difference between a dose causing euphoria and a dose causing fatal respiratory depression is very small."
He said fentanyl   was   colourless, odourless and often tasteless in very small amounts, making contamination difficult to detect.
Mustafa warned that vape devices allowed rapid delivery of substances into the lungs and bloodstream, meaning users could experience sudden toxicity with little warning if fentanyl or other synthetic psychoactive substances were added into vape liquids.
He said this increased risks of overdose, seizures, respiratory failure and sudden death.
Earlier, National Anti-Drug Agency   director-general Datuk Ruslin Jusoh said specialised test kits would be rolled out next month to detect fentanyl and emerging synthetic substances amid growing concerns over drug-laced vape liquids.
He said authorities had, prior to this, not had the capacity to check for such drugs in vape liquids during raids.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US Treasury chief threatens to sanction Oman if it joins Hormuz tolls
Article:
WASHINGTON: US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Thursday threatened to target ally Oman if it helped impose a tolling system in the key Strait of Hormuz, warning of sanctions against all parties involved in such actions.
"Oman, in particular, should know that the US Treasury will aggressively target any actors involved – directly or indirectly – in facilitating tolls for the Strait and any willing partners will be penalized," Bessent warned in a post on X.
He said the United States would "not tolerate any effort to impose a tolling system in the Strait of Hormuz," a reference to Iranian proposals to do so as part of a potential agreement with Washington.
Bessent later told a White House press briefing that he spoke with the Omani ambassador earlier Thursday, and received assurances that "there were no plans for tolling" the critical waterway.
"I told him that this was a non-starter and he did not want to risk either the Oman individuals or Omani financial institutions getting sanctioned," Bessent said.
The strait, through which about a fifth of the world's energy supplies normally passes, has been a flashpoint since the United States and Israel launched the war on Iran in late February.
Teheran's retaliatory actions have engulfed the region in violence and seen Iran virtually block the strait, sending global energy and fertilizer prices soaring.
Oman had mediated talks between Washington and Teheran in Geneva before the war, and has itself come under attack from Iran.
On Wednesday, US President Donald Trump appeared to threaten Oman when asked about a possible short-term arrangement allowing it and Iran to control Hormuz, saying he would "blow them up" if Muscat did so.
The White House did not immediately respond when asked by AFP if Trump had misspoken and had intended to refer to Iran rather than its ally Oman.
Thursday's threat by Bessent came hours after the Treasury sanctioned Iran's "Persian Gulf Strait Authority," Teheran's new agency that collects fees for transiting the strait.
Washington extended the threat of sanctions to anyone paying the fees, because they "may be providing support to and receiving services from" Iran's Revolutionary Guards, and therefore may "be exposed to sanctions risk."
On Thursday, the United States and Iran accused each other of violating an ongoing truce following an exchange of fire, the most serious salvos since they agreed to a ceasefire in April.
The two countries have been locked in weeks-long indirect talks through mediators to bring the conflict to an end, so far to no avail.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: EU sanctions seven Israeli settlers, organisations
Article:
BRUSSELS, Belgium: The EU formally sanctioned seven Israeli settlers and organisations on Thursday over violence against Palestinians and settlement expansion in the Israeli-occupied West Bank.
On May 11, European Union foreign ministers agreed on the sanctions which had been stalled by Hungary's former prime minister Viktor Orban but his ouster paved the way for the veto to be lifted.
The sanctions consist of travel bans and asset freezes, and EU citizens and companies are also banned from making funds, financial assets or other economic resources available to those listed.
The sanctioned individuals included Daniella Weiss, a leading figure in the settler movement.
The EU also sanctioned 10 representatives of the Palestinian militant group Hamas, the European Council, representing the 27 member states, said.
The occupied West Bank has been gripped by almost daily violence involving Israeli troops and settlers since the start of the Gaza war in October 2023.
The EU has also come under pressure by Spain and Italy to sanction Israel's National Security Minister Itamar Ben Gvir, who posted a video showing detained activists from a Gaza-bound flotilla forced to their knees with hands bound.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang expands list of heritage items to 50
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
Fort Cornwallis, asam laksa and char kway teow are among the latest additions to Penang's list of cultural heritage, as the state continues the push to gazette its multicultural legacy.
The gazettements on May 7 bring the number of items under the Penang State Heritage Enactment 2011 to 50, comprising 15 historical sites, seven intangible cultural heritage and 28 dishes.
New landmarks added to the list are Penang Free School, Penang High Court, Kapitan Keling Mosque, Acheen Street Malay Mosque, Khoo Kongsi, the Penang State Museum Board building and St George's Church.
Penang Tourism and Creative Economy Committee chairman Wong Hon Wai yesterday said the recognitions were a major milestone in the state's cultural preservation efforts.
"Although the Penang State Heritage Enactment 2011 was passed in 2011, no official state-level cultural heritage gazettement had been carried out under the enactment over the past 15 years.
"As such, the Penang government took proactive and important steps this year to translate the legal provisions into concrete action."
On Jan 22, the state gazetted several intangible cultural heritage elements, including the nasi kandar and kopitiam culture, the Thaipusam and Chingay processions, St Anne's Feast in Bukit Mertajam and the Penang Tanjong dialect.
Also in the list were traditional dishes such as "nasi kandar", "pasembor", "oh chien", "Hokkien mee", "mi udang" and "roti Benggali".
Wong said the gazettement reflected the state's commitment to preserving Penang's historical memory, cultural diversity, artistic heritage and social identity.
He said the recognition of the heritage of various ethnic communities reflected Penang's culture of openness and coexistence.
"Heritage is not merely a repository of local memories, but also an important bridge in strengthening national unity."
Wong added that all items on the list would be proposed for recognition under the National Heritage Act and nominated for the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organisation's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.
He said the initiative would strengthen Penang's standing as an international cultural heritage tourism destination and support growth in sectors such as tourism, education and research as well as creative economy.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NHRA seeks tenancy act
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
The National House Rental Association (NHRA) has urged the government to expedite the enactment and implementation of a residential tenancy act (RTA) to protect homeowners from being penalised for illegal activities carried out by tenants.
NHRA president Prakash P. Kalivanan highlighted growing public concern over residential properties being used for illicit operations, including Bitcoin mining, prostitution, harbouring undocumented immigrants, storing stolen goods and loan-sharking activities.
He said under current legal provisions, property owners could face investigation or legal action if authorities determined they had knowledge of the unlawful activities or failed to take reasonable preventative steps.
"A majority of homeowners rent out their properties in good faith and are often unaware of the activities carried out by their tenants.
"Many rely on rental income to meet their financial commitments. They should not be unfairly victimised due to the actions of tenants acting without their knowledge."
Prakash said the Housing and Local Government Ministry should deliver a comprehensive RTA without further delay.
"The legislation is crucial in establishing a balanced, transparent, and legally secure rental ecosystem."
He said this would also regulate the residential market by outlining the explicit rights, responsibilities and obligations of all parties involved, minimising disputes and improving enforcement mechanisms.
Prakash said landlords should exercise reasonable diligence and take proactive measures to shield themselves from potential legal complications.
He added that property owners must ensure they prepared a proper, legally stamped, written tenancy agreement that explicitly prohibited tenants from conducting illegal activities on the premises.
"Landlords are strongly encouraged to carry out basic tenant verification and background checks, including verifying identification documents and ensuring all personal details provided are authentic, before handing over the keys.
"Furthermore, they should conduct periodic inspections of the rented property after giving reasonable notice to ensure the premises are being used strictly for lawful residential purposes."
Prakash said maintaining documentation, such as copies of tenant identification, rental payment records and written correspondence, was vital to demonstrate that the landlord acted responsibly, should any issue arise.
He said landlords must also report any suspicious or illegal activity on their property to the authorities immediately.
"Prompt action will prevent further misuse of the premises and protect the homeowner from possible legal implications," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: TM accelerating AI and digital innovation capabilities
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Telekom Malaysia Bhd (TM) reaffirmed that it is on track to becoming a digital powerhouse by 2030 through the disciplined execution of its PWR 2030 strategy.
Managing director and group chief executive officer Amar Huzaimi Md Deris said TM is entering a new growth phase centred on artificial intelligence (AI), digital innovation and sustainability.
"To strengthen how we create value, we are accelerating AI and digital innovation capabilities while embedding sustainability more deeply across our business, operations and strategic investments," he said in a statement.
In 2025, TM distributed about RM2 billion in shareholder and economic value.
TM has been expanding its AI ecosystem through AI-ready data centres, graphics processing unit-as-a-service offerings, cloud infrastructure and cybersecurity solutions.
Of the RM2 billion distributed, about RM1.2 billion was returned to shareholders through dividends, equivalent to 31 sen per share. A further RM750 million was contributed through taxes, zakat and socioeconomic initiatives, TM said in a statement.
TM also reiterated its revised dividend policy of a minimum payout ratio of 75 per cent of net profit.
Beginning from the first quarter of 2026, dividends will be declared and paid on a quarterly basis.
In 2025, TM recorded a revenue growth of 11.4 per cent to RM11.9 billion, while pre-tax profit stood at RM2 billion and operating cash flow was at RM2.5 billion.
Total shareholders' return stood at 23.5 per cent, alongside a 21 per cent increase in share price.
On the sustainability front, TM generated RM135 million in revenue from low-carbon products and services in 2025 while reducing carbon emissions by 34 per cent compared with its 2019 baseline.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australia charges woman over Islamic State links
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
SYDNEY: Police yesterday charged a woman linked to the Islamic State (IS) group with membership of a terrorist organisation and entering a known conflict zone.
Both charges carry a maximum penalty of up to 10 years in prison.
Hundreds of women from Western nations were lured to the Middle East as IS gained prominence in the early 2010s, in many cases following husbands who had signed up as militants.
A joint counter-terror police team said they would allege that the woman, 34, travelled to Syria between 2013 and 2014 with a man to join IS.
The man was now believed to be imprisoned in the Middle East, they added.
The woman was detained by Kurdish forces in 2019 and held in the al-Hawl Internally Displaced Persons camp until returning to Australia last September, they said.
Her arrest follows the return to Australia this month of a number of women and children linked to suspected IS fighters.
Two women —  a mother and a daughter —  were arrested on arrival in Melbourne.
Police accused them of having kept a woman as a slave after travelling to Syria in 2014 to support the group.
A third woman was also arrested on arrival here and charged with entering a restricted area and joining a terrorist organisation.
This week, 13 more IS-linked Australians — four women and their nine children — flew home from Syria.
In a statement following their landing, federal police said none of the cohort had been charged with an offence.
Australia's Human Rights Commission urged the government in March to help repatriate its citizens still in Syria.
But others have accused the women of turning their back on Australia and believe they should be left to face the consequences.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RON95 subsidy adjustment is last resort, says govt adviser
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Any move to adjust RON95 petrol subsidies will be a last resort for the government due to its direct impact on household spending, according to a senior economic adviser in the Prime Minister's Office.
Prime Minister's Office senior economic and finance adviser Nurhisham Hussein said Putrajaya currently has several policy options available but is proceeding cautiously given the sensitivity surrounding fuel prices and broader cost-of-living pressures.
Nurhisham spoke on the Keluar Sekejap podcast hosted by former health minister Khairy Jamaluddin and former Umno information chief Shahril Hamdan which aired on YouTube yesterday.
Speaking about the fuel shortage as a result of the global oil uncertainties involving the conflict at Straits of Hormuz ship route, he said the government had already reduced the subsidised fuel quota from 300 litres to 200 litres monthly, which he claimed only affected about 10 per cent of consumers in the country.
"About 90 per cent of consumers using fuel in Malaysia are not affected by the 200-litre RON95 quota," he said speaking to Khairy on the show.
Nurhisham said among the policy options available to the government were increasing the subsidised fuel price from RM1.99 per litre to RM2.05 or RM2.10, or further reducing the monthly quota, which he added was "a last option".
He said that any rationalisation involving petrol subsidies would likely be deferred for as long as possible.
"Petrol is a very sensitive issue and it has a direct impact on the rakyat's pockets.
